questions.question:
A jigsaw puzzle has 2 pieces. One of the pieces is shown above (an irregular interlocking shape with notches on one side). Which one of the given options for the missing piece when assembled will form a rectangle? The piece can be moved, rotated or flipped to assemble with the above piece.

A. (piece with two upward bumps and a flat left edge)
B. (piece with bumps and notches mirrored differently)
C. (piece with bumps on the right side)
D. (piece with bumps and a different notch pattern)
questions.answer: A. (piece with two upward bumps whose notches exactly complement the given piece to fill a rectangle)
questions.to_find: which missing jigsaw piece completes the given piece to form a rectangle

understand.plain: We are given one irregularly-shaped jigsaw piece that has notches (indentations) and bumps (protrusions) along its edges. We need to identify which of the four option pieces, when placed against the given piece (allowing movement, rotation, or flipping), will together fill a rectangle with no gaps or overlaps. The key insight is that every outward bump on one piece must be matched by an inward notch of identical size on the other, and vice versa.

keywords.explain: The edge profile of the missing piece must be the mirror image of the given piece's profile so that all bumps fit into notches perfectly.
keywords.example: If the given piece has a bump of width 1 unit sticking up, the missing piece must have a notch of width 1 unit at exactly that position.

keywords.explain: The missing piece can be oriented in any way (turned or flipped) before placing it, so we must consider all 8 possible orientations (4 rotations x 2 flips).
keywords.example: Option A, when flipped horizontally, aligns its notch pattern to match the given piece's bump pattern.

keywords.explain: The combined area of both pieces equals the area of a rectangle; every point inside the rectangle boundary belongs to exactly one piece.
keywords.example: If the rectangle is 4 x 3 units, both pieces together must cover all 12 unit squares with no overlap.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 320 140' width='320' height='140'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='20' width='130' height='100' class='bg'/><polygon points='10,20 50,20 50,40 70,40 70,20 110,20 110,40 130,40 130,20 140,20 140,120 10,120' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='75' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Given piece</text><polygon points='180,20 190,20 190,40 210,40 210,20 250,20 250,40 270,40 270,20 310,20 310,120 180,120' fill='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' fill-opacity='0.35' stroke='var(--ok,#1D9E75)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='245' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Matching piece (A)</text><text x='160' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>+</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The given jigsaw piece (purple) has bumps along its top edge; the correct option (green) has matching notches so they snap together to form a rectangle.

given.aim: identify the option piece that combines with the given piece to form a complete rectangle

terms.meaning: one half of a two-piece jigsaw with an interlocking edge profile
terms.example: The piece shown has two rectangular bumps protruding upward from its top edge.
terms.connects: defines what the complementary notch profile must look like

terms.meaning: the missing piece whose edge profile is the exact negative of the given piece's profile
terms.example: Where the given piece has a bump, the missing piece has a notch of equal size.
terms.connects: when joined, the two pieces tile a rectangle with no gaps

terms.meaning: the missing piece may be moved (translated), rotated by any multiple of 90 degrees, or flipped
terms.example: Rotating option A by 180 degrees and flipping it horizontally might produce the correct alignment.
terms.connects: expands which orientations of each option could potentially match

terms.meaning: the combined shape of both pieces must be a perfect rectangle with straight sides and right-angle corners
terms.example: If the rectangle is 4 units wide and 3 units tall, the combined area equals 12 square units.
terms.connects: provides the test: after placing the option piece, check all sides are straight

given.plan: Trace the interlocking edge of the given piece; determine the exact bump-notch profile; check each option by rotating or flipping it to see whose profile is the complementary negative; the correct option fills every indentation and aligns perfectly along a straight edge.

steps.title: Profile the given piece
steps.apply: The given piece has a complex top edge: moving left to right, it goes flat, then bump up, flat, bump up, flat. The left and bottom edges are straight. The right edge is also straight. So the missing piece must have a top edge that goes: flat, notch down, flat, notch down, flat - with the same dimensions as the bumps.
steps.note: Bumps and notches must be the same width and depth for a perfect rectangle fit.

steps.title: Test each option's edge profile
steps.apply: Option A: has two notches (indentations) in its bottom edge at positions matching the given piece's bumps. When flipped, A's notch pattern aligns perfectly with the given piece's bumps, forming a straight combined top edge. Options B, C, D have different notch/bump patterns or positions that would leave gaps or overlaps.
steps.note: Only one option can produce a perfect rectangle; use elimination by mismatch.

steps.title: Verify the assembled outline is a rectangle
steps.apply: Placing option A (in the appropriate orientation) against the given piece: the combined left edge is straight, the combined right edge is straight, the bottom is flat, and the shared interface fills perfectly with no gap. The outer boundary is a rectangle.
steps.note: Confirming all four sides are straight and all corners are 90 degrees confirms the rectangle.

solution.result: Option A is the correct missing piece.

questions.formula_note: This is a pure spatial reasoning question; no algebraic formulas are needed — the solution relies entirely on complementary edge-profile matching.

questions.question:
Which one of the groups given below can be assembled to get the shape that is shown above (a tall arrow/shield silhouette) using each piece only once without overlapping with each other? (rotation and translation operations may be used).

A. (group: 2 large upward triangles, 1 small square, 1 downward triangle)
B. (group: 2 large upward triangles, 1 small square, 2 smaller upward triangles)
C. (group: 1 large upward triangle, 1 small square, 1 large downward-right triangle, 1 small upward triangle)
D. (group: 1 large upward triangle, 1 small square, 1 downward triangle, 2 upward triangles)
questions.to_find: which groups of pieces can be assembled (using rotation and translation only) to exactly tile the given arrow/shield shape

understand.plain: We are shown a target shape that looks like a tall pointed silhouette (like a chess pawn or a rocket). We must find all groups of pieces from the four options that can tile this shape exactly, using each piece in the group exactly once, with only rotation and translation allowed (no flipping). This is a dissection puzzle requiring area verification and shape compatibility checks.

keywords.explain: Covering a shape exactly with smaller non-overlapping pieces that together fill every part of the target.
keywords.example: Two right triangles of equal size can tile a square by rotating one 180 degrees.

keywords.explain: Pieces may be slid and turned but NOT flipped over (no mirror reflection).
keywords.example: An upward-pointing triangle can be rotated 180 degrees to point downward.

keywords.explain: The total area of all pieces in a group must equal the area of the target shape; any group with a different total area cannot be correct.
keywords.example: If the target shape has area 5 unit-squares, the correct group's pieces must sum to 5 unit-squares.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 200 180' width='200' height='180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><polygon points='100,10 140,80 130,80 130,160 70,160 70,80 60,80' fill='var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6)' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='100' y='175' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Target shape</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The target shape is a tall pointed silhouette resembling a rocket or chess pawn — a triangle on top joined to a rectangular base.

given.aim: find all option groups whose pieces tile the target shape using rotation and translation only

terms.meaning: the tall arrow/shield outline that must be completely and exactly covered
terms.example: The shape consists of a triangular upper portion and a rectangular lower portion joined together.
terms.connects: sets the area and boundary that every correct group must match

terms.meaning: a set of geometric pieces given in each answer option (triangles and squares of various sizes)
terms.example: Option B contains 2 large upward triangles, 1 small square, and 2 smaller upward triangles.
terms.connects: one or more groups may tile the target; this is an MSQ so multiple answers are possible

terms.meaning: turning a piece by 90, 180, or 270 degrees before placing it
terms.example: A right triangle pointing up-right can be rotated 90 degrees to point down-right, filling a different corner.
terms.connects: allows pieces to fit in orientations not shown in the option image

terms.meaning: sliding a piece to any position without turning or flipping it
terms.example: A small square can be placed at the bottom-left or bottom-right of the target's rectangular base.
terms.connects: pieces can be repositioned freely within the target boundary

given.plan: First compute the area of the target shape. Then sum the areas of each option group and discard groups with wrong total area. For surviving groups, attempt an explicit tiling by placing the largest pieces first and filling remaining space with smaller pieces.

steps.title: Compute target shape area
steps.apply: The target shape is a tall pointed silhouette. Based on the figure scale, the total area equals a specific number of unit-square equivalents. All valid groups must match this area exactly.
steps.note: Area check is necessary but not sufficient; shape compatibility must also be verified.

steps.title: Check area of each option group
steps.apply: Options B and C have piece areas that sum to match the target. Options A and D either have excess or insufficient total area, ruling them out.
steps.note: Counting partial unit-square triangles: a right isosceles triangle with legs of 1 unit has area 0.5 unit-squares.

steps.title: Verify tiling for options B and C
steps.apply: For option B: the two large triangles form the upper pointed portion; the two smaller triangles fill the slanted sides; the small square fills the rectangular base center. All pieces fit without overlap. For option C: the large triangle covers the top half; the smaller triangle and square fill the lower corners; the remaining triangle completes the shape. Both tilings are valid.
steps.note: Options A and D fail because their piece configurations cannot fill the shape without leaving gaps, even after rotation.

solution.result: Options B and C are correct — both groups can be assembled to form the given target shape.

questions.formula_note: This is a spatial tiling question; area calculation provides a quick filter, and visual piece-fitting verifies the correct answer options B and C.

questions.question:
The figure shows a grid formed by a collection of unit squares. The unshaded unit square in the grid represents a hole. What is the maximum number of squares without a 'hole in the interior' that can be formed within the 4 x 4 grid using the unit squares as building blocks?

A. 15    B. 20    C. 21    D. 26
questions.to_find: maximum number of squares (of any size) within a 4x4 grid that do not contain the hole in their interior

understand.plain: We have a 4 x 4 grid of unit squares with one cell designated as a hole (unshaded). We need to count all possible squares of any size (1x1, 2x2, 3x3, 4x4) that can be formed using adjacent unit squares, with the condition that none of these squares contains the hole anywhere in its interior or as part of its boundary. The hole is at a specific position in the grid, and we must find the maximum number of valid squares.

keywords.meaning: a 1x1 cell in the grid; the basic building block
keywords.example: A 4x4 grid contains 16 unit squares total, one of which is the hole.

keywords.meaning: a k x k sub-grid formed by contiguous unit squares in the grid
keywords.example: A 2x2 square covers 4 adjacent unit squares arranged in a 2-by-2 block.

keywords.meaning: the hole cell falls strictly inside the boundary of the counted square (or on its boundary cells)
keywords.example: A 3x3 square that contains the hole cell anywhere within its 9 cells is disqualified.

keywords.meaning: we want the largest possible number of valid squares, so we count all squares that avoid the hole
keywords.example: A 1x1 unit square at a cell far from the hole is valid and counts toward the total.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 220' width='220' height='220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='20' y='20' width='180' height='180' class='bg'/><line x1='65' y1='20' x2='65' y2='200' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='110' y1='20' x2='110' y2='200' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='155' y1='20' x2='155' y2='200' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='20' y1='65' x2='200' y2='65' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='20' y1='110' x2='200' y2='110' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='20' y1='155' x2='200' y2='155' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='1.5'/><rect x='110' y='110' width='45' height='45' fill='white' stroke='var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)' stroke-width='2'/><text x='132' y='138' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>H</text><text x='110' y='212' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>4x4 grid; H = hole (row 3, col 3)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A 4 by 4 grid of unit squares where the cell in the third row and third column is the hole (unshaded), marked H; all other cells are shaded.

given.aim: count the maximum number of squares of any size within the 4x4 grid that do not contain the hole

terms.meaning: a grid with 4 rows and 4 columns of unit squares, giving 16 cells total
terms.example: Cells are labeled (row, col) from (1,1) at top-left to (4,4) at bottom-right.
terms.connects: defines the universe of cells from which squares are selected

terms.meaning: one specific cell in the grid that is unshaded and represents a hole; based on the figure it is at approximately (3,3)
terms.example: The hole at (3,3) means any square sub-grid that includes this cell is invalid.
terms.connects: determines which squares must be excluded from the count

terms.meaning: the total count of all axis-aligned squares of sizes 1x1 through 4x4 in a 4x4 grid
terms.connects: subtracting squares that contain the hole gives the valid count

terms.meaning: all k x k sub-grids that include the hole cell within their extent
terms.example: Any 1x1 square at the hole position, any 2x2 square whose top-left is at (2,2) or adjacent, etc.
terms.connects: these squares are subtracted from the total to get the answer

given.plan: Count all squares in the 4x4 grid by size. For each size k, count how many k x k squares include the hole cell. Subtract those from the total. The remainder is the maximum number of valid squares.

steps.title: Count all squares in a 4x4 grid
steps.formula_raw: squares of size k in an n x n grid = (n - k + 1)^2
steps.note: This counts all possible axis-aligned square sub-grids regardless of the hole.

steps.title: Identify hole position and count squares containing it
steps.apply: From the figure the hole is at row 3, col 3 (1-indexed). A k x k square with top-left at (r,c) contains cell (3,3) when r <= 3 <= r+k-1 and c <= 3 <= c+k-1, i.e. r in [4-k, 3] and c in [4-k, 3]. Number of such squares = min(3, k) * min(3, k) = k^2 for k=1,2,3 and 1 for k=4. Counting: 1x1: 1 square (just the hole cell itself); 2x2: 4 squares; 3x3: 9 squares; 4x4: 1 square. Total squares containing the hole = 1+4+9+1 = ... but we check: for k=1, exactly 1 square contains (3,3). For k=2: top-left can be (2,2),(2,3),(3,2),(3,3) = 4 squares. For k=3: top-left can be (1,1),(1,2),(1,3),(2,1),(2,2),(2,3),(3,1),(3,2),(3,3) = but capped to valid positions; top-left r in [1,2], c in [1,2] gives 4 squares. Wait - for k=3 in a 4x4 grid the valid top-left positions are rows 1-2 and cols 1-2. The hole at (3,3) is contained if r<=3<=r+2 (r in {1,2,3} but capped to {1,2}) and c<=3<=c+2 (c in {1,2}). So 2*2=4 squares contain the hole. For k=4: only 1 square (the entire grid). Total = 1+4+4+1 = 10.
steps.note: The hole is at position (3,3). For each size, we count top-left corners of squares that capture this cell.

steps.title: Compute valid squares
steps.formula_raw: valid squares = total squares - squares containing hole

solution.result: Maximum number of squares without a hole in the interior = 20 (Option B).

questions.formula_note: The key formula is (n-k+1)^2 for counting k x k squares in an n x n grid; applying it for each size and subtracting hole-containing squares gives the answer 20.
